revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Chintaku Reifu]][[ja:鎮宅霊符]]
{{Otheruses|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|qq=}} '''Chintaku Reifu''' (鎮宅霊符) is a set of 72 types of {{ill|Omamori|ja|護符|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Omamori|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to お守り|1=Omamori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1756788}}, also known as "Taijo Secret Method Chintaku Reifu" or "Chintaku 72-Way Reifu." The deity presiding over these talismans is called '''Chintaku Reifu-shin''' (鎮宅霊符神). == Overview == {{More citations needed|date=December 2024|section=1|qq=}} The talismans originate from ancient China and are featured in {{ill|Daozang|en|Daozang|qid=Q1141990|lt=Daozang|1=Daozang}}, the Taoist canon, under the title "Taijo Secret Method Chintaku Reifu." According to legend, as referenced in the {{ill|Shangyuan Jing|ja|上元経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangyuan Jing|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the talismans were given to a man named Liu Jinping, who lived in a place considered to have bad geomancy known as the "House of the Three Fools." He received these talismans from an immortal as a reward for his hospitality. Emperor Wen of the Han Dynasty later popularized them. In Japan, the talismans are said to have been introduced by Prince Rimyo of Baekje during the reign of Empress Suiko. However, it is widely believed they actually arrived in the medieval period. In Japan, the talismans were particularly integrated into {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myoken Bosatsu|1=Myōken}} worship and became associated with Buddhism, {{ill|Onmyōdō|en|Onmyōdō|qid=Q1340540|lt=Onmyōdō|1=Onmyōdō}}, and Shinto. Over time, Chintaku Reifu-shin also became worshiped as a deity. The deity's iconography often features Chintaku Reifu-shin in the center, flanked by the attendants Houke-dōji and Shike-dōrō, influenced by depictions of {{ill|Black Turtle-Snake|en|Black Turtle-Snake|qid=Q125696|lt=Black Turtle-Snake|1=Black Turtle-Snake}}. In the early modern period, the 72 talismans were often arranged in a mandala-like form on a single sheet of paper for worship. Figures like {{ill|Katō Kiyomasa|en|Katō Kiyomasa|qid=Q1069843|lt=Katō Kiyomasa|1=Katō Kiyomasa}} and {{ill|Kusunoki Masashige|en|Kusunoki Masashige|qid=Q382234|lt=Kusunoki Masashige|1=Kusunoki Masashige}} were devoted followers. Mirrors and bells dedicated to this deity have also been preserved. == Temples and Shrines == === Temples === * {{ill|Shorinzan Daruma Temple|en|Shorinzan Daruma Temple|qid=Q11641897|lt=Daruma-dera (Takasaki)|1=Shorinzan Daruma Temple}} (Gunma Prefecture, Takasaki City)<ref>[https://www.daruma.or.jp/ご祈祷/ Official Website of Daruma-dera]</ref> * {{ill|Ake-no-dera|ja|曙寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ake-no-dera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City)<ref>[http://www.kangaan.jp/kangaan.html Official Website]</ref> * {{ill|Gyogan-ji|ja|行願寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gyogan-ji|qq=}} (Chintaku Reifu-shin Hall, Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City)<ref name="#1">[http://satokei59.web.fc2.com/ChintakuMyouken.html Chintaku Reifu-shin]</ref> * Shorenzan Fudō-dō Myōō-in (Kyoto Prefecture, Kyoto City)<ref name="#1"/> * {{ill|Hōon'in|en|Hōon'in|qid=Q11428052|lt=Hōon'in|1=Hōon'in}} (Osaka Prefecture, Osaka City)<ref name="#1"/> * {{ill|Myōhō-ji (Osaka)|ja|妙法寺 (大阪市東成区)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Myōhō-ji (Osaka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka Prefecture, Osaka City)<ref>[http://www.city.osaka.lg.jp/higashinari/page/0000000433.html Osaka City Website]</ref> * {{ill|Jōfuku-in|ja|成福院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōfuku-in|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture, Heguri Town)<ref>[http://www.jyofukuin.jp/ Official Website]</ref> === Shrines === * {{ill|Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|lt=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto|lt=Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto Honcho|ja|天社土御門神道本庁|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tensha Tsuchimikado Shinto Honcho|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天社土御門神道}} (Fukui Prefecture, Ōi Town)<ref name="#1"/> * {{ill|Hoshida Myōken Shrine|ja|星田妙見宮|3=小松神社_(交野市)|1=Hoshida Myōkengū|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshida Myōken Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Hoshida Myōkengū|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 小松神社 (交野市)}} (Osaka Prefecture, Katano City)<ref>[https://www.hoshida-myoken.com Official Website of Hoshida Myōken Shrine]</ref> * {{ill|Osaka Tenmangū|en|Osaka Tenmangū|qid=Q385793|lt=Osaka Tenmangū|1=Osaka Tenmangū}} (Reifu-sha, Osaka Prefecture, Osaka City)<ref name="#1"/> * {{ill|Horikoshi Shrine|lt=Horikoshi Shrine|lt=Horyo Shrine|ja|堀越神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horyo Shrine|qq=}} (Osaka Prefecture, Osaka City) * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine (Higashiosaka)|ja|鎮宅霊符神社 (東大阪市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu Shrine (Higashiosaka)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Osaka Prefecture, Higashiosaka City)<ref name="#1"/> * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu Shrine (Nara)|qq=}} (Nara Prefecture, Nara City)<ref name="#1"/> * {{ill|Reifu Shrine (Yatsushiro)|ja|八代神社#霊符神社|1=Yatsushiro_Shrine#Reifu_Shrine|lt=Reifu Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reifu Shrine (Yatsushiro)|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsushiro_Shrine#Reifu_Shrine|qq=}} (Kumamoto Prefecture, Yatsushiro City)<ref name="#1"/>
== Notes == {{Reflist|qq=}} == References == {{Cite book|title=Simple Dictionary of Taoism|author=Huang Haide|publisher=Sichuan University Press|year=1991|qq=}} {{Cite book|title=Daoist Ritual Practices|author=Lü Yuanshu|publisher=Quelling the Home|date=Unknown|qq=}} * Tadao Sakai (Ed.), ''Research on Japanese and Chinese Religious Culture,'' Heian Publishing, 1991. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chintaku Reifu Shrine|qq=}} * {{ill|Myōken|en|Myōken|qid=Q10960623|lt=Myōken|1=Myōken}} ==References== {{reflist|qq=}}
